turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show. Today we're diving into something that affects all of us—how AI systems are quietly consuming our personal data, and whether current laws can actually protect us. So, where exactly is the biggest tension right now?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: The core tension is that AI needs massive data to perform well, but privacy laws assume data can be controlled and deleted. Take the right to be forgotten—GDPR says you can delete your data, but neural networks store information in distributed, entangled patterns. You can't surgically remove one person's contribution.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So if someone requests their data be deleted, companies essentially can't fully comply? That seems like a massive legal gap. How are regulators and companies actually grappling with this impossible-sounding requirement?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Honestly, they're mostly improvising. Some retrain models from scratch without the flagged

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Retraining from scratch sounds incredibly expensive. Is that actually scalable, or are we heading toward a world where companies just accept the legal risk and hope they don't get caught?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: It's not scalable at all. And the memorization problem makes this worse—a 2021 study showed GPT-2 could be coaxed into regurgitating names, phone numbers, and addresses verbatim. You can't just delete data that's baked into billions of parameters. Some companies are absolutely betting on regulatory enforcement lagging behind the technology.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So as AI capabilities outpace our ability to protect personal data, what gives you hope—are there technical solutions on the horizon, or are we just waiting for a privacy disaster to force real change?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: There are technical approaches being researched—differential privacy adds statistical noise to training data
